response.description_long:
In-house legal teams are buried in routine contracts—NDAs, MSAs, DPAs—that pile up as the sales queue grows. Every NDA that sits for days is a deal that cools, a sales rep who escalates, a general counsel who gets asked why legal is the bottleneck. The work is 80 percent the same negotiation the team ran last week, but it still burns evenings or ships to outside counsel at hundreds of dollars an hour.

Briefhound automates first-pass redlining of commercial contracts by encoding a legal team's negotiation playbook—preferred positions, fallback language, and walk-away terms for each clause type. Language models classify each clause, compare it to the playbook, and propose tracked changes with margin explanations citing the applied position. Lawyers review in Microsoft Word, accepting or adjusting edits, and the playbook learns from every change. The entire workflow stays inside Word; there is no proprietary editor to learn.

Teams start from Briefhound's library of standard positions covering the routine commercial stack: limitation of liability, indemnification, data protection, payment terms, termination, and more. A typical first playbook is encoded in an afternoon working session with Briefhound's legal knowledge engineers. Playbooks are versioned, so precedent is documented rather than folklore. When a lawyer consistently edits proposals—say, accepting a carve-out nine times—Briefhound suggests an update to the playbook, which takes effect only upon approval.

Contracts arrive via upload, email, or an intake form. An approvals dashboard shows every contract in flight, cycle times per stage, and which clauses stall negotiations. The system reports cycle-time analytics, giving general counsel the metric they actually report upward. After rollout, legal teams report first-pass review in under 10 minutes and NDA turnaround cut from days to same-day. Routine NDAs increasingly close without a lawyer touching them beyond a summary scan.

Briefhound is priced as a per-seat annual subscription with unlimited documents—the per-document price would tax the volume problem the product exists to solve. Seats are for the legal team; sales submitters are free. Implementation, the playbook session, and support are included. The company's arithmetic: an in-house lawyer's fully loaded hour against a $600 outside-counsel hour against software that returns a first-pass redline in minutes. A team routing forty contracts a month to outside counsel is spending mid-six figures annually on paper the playbook already knows how to negotiate.

Founded in Boston in 2021 by former in-house counsel Claire Donnelly and NLP engineers Raj Iyer and Tom Okonkwo, Briefhound has 44 people, roughly half former practicing lawyers or legal-ops professionals. The company is SOC 2 Type II certified, with isolated customer data and models fine-tuned per customer. Briefhound presents at CLOC and regional legal ops gatherings, and its aggregate playbook statistics have been cited in legal ops publications. The Word-native design decision is regularly credited in customer interviews as the reason adoption stuck with skeptical senior counsel.
